shared_strengths.pattern: Strong PoV design across all three solutions
shared_strengths.detail: Each PoV has a clear Go/No-Go deliverable: 6R classification report (migration), TCO comparison report (SAP), BSI-C5 gap report (sovereign). These are concrete procurement artifacts, not vague 'pilot' descriptions.

shared_strengths.pattern: Consistent cost model with delivery_defaults alignment
shared_strengths.detail: All three solutions use identical role rates matching portfolio.json delivery_defaults (SA 1800, IE 1200, PM 1400 EUR/day). Assumptions are specific and auditable. Bill of materials includes tooling and infrastructure.

shared_strengths.pattern: Tier pricing progression is commercially coherent
shared_strengths.detail: No inverted tiers. Price jumps reflect meaningful scope increases (department → enterprise). PoV pricing under 50K EUR allows departmental approval without board escalation at large utilities.
Implementation phases map clearly to proposition DOES statements. Each phase contributes to delivering the promised outcome.

shared_weaknesses.pattern: OT/IT integration is the universal blind spot
shared_weaknesses.detail: All three solutions touch SCADA, Netzleitstellen, or OT systems in their scope descriptions but none specify the actual integration approach. For energy utilities, OT/IT convergence is the #1 pain point (listed in market definition). This is the most likely failure mode in procurement evaluation — the CISO and OT-Leiter will reject any solution that doesn't explicitly address OT boundaries.
shared_weaknesses.recommendation: Add a cross-portfolio OT integration assumption: either define a standard OT/IT boundary approach that all solutions reference, or explicitly scope OT systems out with a separate engagement path.

shared_weaknesses.pattern: No competitive positioning in any solution
shared_weaknesses.detail: All three solutions lack competitor context. This is the same market (large energy utilities) where procurement teams benchmark aggressively. Cloud migration competes with Accenture/Capgemini, SAP with RISE/Atos/IBM, sovereign cloud with Ionos/STACKIT/Delos. The absence is consistent, suggesting no competitor entities exist in the project.
shared_weaknesses.recommendation: Create competitor entities for this market and run repricing flow. This is portfolio-level work, not per-solution.

shared_weaknesses.pattern: Implementation phase durations are fixed, not per-tier
shared_weaknesses.detail: All three solutions describe implementation phases with durations that map to roughly the Small tier (11-18 weeks), but tier scopes range from 11 to 40 weeks. A Provider SA planning the Large tier delivery would need to re-estimate all phase durations. This is a structural issue with the batch-generated solution format.
shared_weaknesses.recommendation: Either add per-tier phase duration tables or add an explicit note that implementation phases describe the baseline (Small) tier and scale proportionally.

shared_weaknesses.pattern: Client SA perspective consistently scores lowest (73-75)
shared_weaknesses.detail: Across all three solutions, the client-side SA finds adoption safety gaps. The pattern: integration feasibility is always 'warn' (OT/IT blind spot), and either vendor lock-in or data/compliance handling has gaps. This suggests the solutions were designed from the provider's delivery perspective, not from the buyer's adoption perspective.
shared_weaknesses.recommendation: Add a standard 'buyer adoption' section to the solution template: integration boundaries, data residency, exit clause, retained team RACI. The SAP solution partially does this (exit clause) but the others don't.

shared_weaknesses.pattern: Small tier margins consistently below or at 30% target
shared_weaknesses.detail: Small tier margins: cloud-migration 29.4%, sap-on-cloud 29.4%, sovereign-cloud 28.8%. All are slightly below the 30% target. This suggests the Small tier is underpriced relative to effort, or effort is slightly overestimated. The pattern is too consistent to be coincidence — likely a systematic pricing calibration issue.
shared_weaknesses.recommendation: Either increase Small tier prices by 3-5% or trim 2-3 person-days from the effort estimates. The easiest fix: reduce PM days in the Small tier by 2 across all solutions.

inconsistencies.pattern: Exit clause present in SAP solution but missing from cloud-migration and sovereign-cloud
inconsistencies.detail: The SAP solution includes a 4-week knowledge transfer exit clause — an excellent practice that the Client SA valued highly. The other two solutions don't include this. For a portfolio selling to the same buyer (large energy utility), this inconsistency is a procurement risk: the buyer will ask 'why does one contract have exit terms and the others don't?'
inconsistencies.recommendation: Add exit clause to cloud-migration and sovereign-cloud assumptions, aligned with the SAP solution's approach.

inconsistencies.detail: Sovereign-cloud references NIS2 as a driver in assumptions. Cloud-migration doesn't mention NIS2. SAP-on-cloud doesn't mention it. For the same market (energy utilities facing April 2026 NIS2 deadline), the regulatory framing should be consistent.
inconsistencies.recommendation: Either reference NIS2 in all three solutions (it affects all of them) or centralize it as a market-level driver that all solutions inherit.

margin_health.summary: Margins are healthy overall but Small tier consistently underperforms the 30% target. PoV margins (12.7-14.3%) are deliberately low for land-and-expand — acceptable. Medium and Large tiers exceed target (30.6-40.0%).

small.assessment: Below 30% target across all solutions — systematic underpricing

large.assessment: Strong — premium margins on enterprise engagements

solution_type_audit.assessment: All three solutions use project type. The product (cloud-services) has no revenue_model field, making project the correct default. No misrouting detected.

upstream_diagnosis.issue: Product entity (cloud-services) is minimal — no revenue_model, pricing_tier, or maturity fields
upstream_diagnosis.impact: Cannot validate solution type routing mechanically. Cannot calibrate pricing against product positioning.
upstream_diagnosis.recommendation: Enrich cloud-services.json with revenue_model: 'project', pricing_tier, and maturity fields.

upstream_diagnosis.issue: No competitor entities exist for this market
upstream_diagnosis.impact: All three solutions lack competitive positioning context. Pricing is internally coherent but externally unvalidated.
upstream_diagnosis.recommendation: Create competitor entities for the energy utility cloud services market (Accenture, Atos, IBM, RISE with SAP, Ionos, STACKIT).

upstream_diagnosis.issue: Feature entities are minimal — name and description only, no readiness field
upstream_diagnosis.impact: Cannot validate feature readiness gate (ga/beta/planned). All features are treated as GA by default.
upstream_diagnosis.recommendation: Add readiness field to all feature JSONs.

priority_actions.action: Add OT/IT integration boundaries across all three solutions
priority_actions.rationale: Universal blind spot flagged by Client SA on all solutions. Energy utility CISO and OT-Leiter will reject without this.
priority_actions.effort: Low — add 1-2 assumptions per solution defining OT scope boundary

priority_actions.action: Add exit clauses to cloud-migration and sovereign-cloud (matching SAP solution pattern)
priority_actions.rationale: Portfolio consistency for same-buyer procurement. SAP solution already has the right template.
priority_actions.effort: Low — copy pattern from SAP solution assumptions

priority_actions.action: Fix Small tier margins across all solutions (currently 28.8-29.4% vs. 30% target)
priority_actions.rationale: Systematic underpricing. Either increase Small prices by 3-5% or reduce PM effort by 2 days.

priority_actions.action: Add per-tier phase duration notes to implementation plans
priority_actions.rationale: Current phase durations only map to Small tier. Provider SAs planning Medium/Large need scaled timelines.
priority_actions.effort: Medium — requires rethinking phase duration structure

priority_actions.action: Create competitor entities and run competitive pricing calibration
priority_actions.rationale: All three solutions lack competitive context. Same market, same procurement team — they will benchmark.

priority_actions.action: Enrich upstream entities (product fields, feature readiness)
priority_actions.rationale: Enables mechanical validation of solution type routing and feature readiness gates.
priority_actions.effort: Low — add 3-4 fields to existing JSONs
